evidence_quotes.quote: Video games are at huge risk of this due to many multiplayer and live-service games made today being so intrinsically linked to the company servers.

paragraphs.text: Greetings! I am writing this as an avid video game consumer and am coming this as referral from Ross Scott of Youtube channel Accursed Farms and the Stop Killing Games initiative. The main notes of the Digital Fairness Act closely border the goals of the Stop Killing Games movement and I think people need some consumer protection from their digital products being taken away from them. Just to be clear this isn't a video game exclusive issue. Plethora of software and even household devices being sold today are at massive risk of being unjustly destroyed/disabled just because their internal sales didn't meet a specific quota. Consumer Rights Wiki is a great website highlighting some of these issues that have already happened. Ex. Bosch Spexor alarms are going to be made virtually unusable by consumers later this year by disabling their cloud services. Why would household alarms need cloud services to be run? Video games are at huge risk of this due to many multiplayer and live-service games made today being so intrinsically linked to the company servers. Anthem is an EA title originally sold for 60 euros (plus 20 if you bought the higher edition of the game) in 2019 is being shut down in 2026. BlackLight Retribution was a game shut down in 2019, albeit being a title that you could play for free, offered content that could only be obtained by purchasing in game microtransactions. Alongside the notes in the initiative like tackling addictive design, dark patterns in the UI etc, adding an anti-disabling/destroying one would also be a huge one. EU citizens deserve better consumer protection in an ever growing digital space. Products are being destroyed and consumers are left exploited constantly and the Digital Fairness Act could be an open door to some great change. If you've read this, thank you, and I hope this initiative goes through.
